analogy: Think of "Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years" through the chapter's own opening images: Map 1 was made in 1154 CE by the Arab geographer Al-Idrisi. The section reproduced here is a detail of the Indian subcontinent from his larger map of the world. Map 2 was made in the 1720s by a French cartographer. The two maps are... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1 INTRODUCTION: TRA..., 1154 CE by the Arab geographer Al-Idrisi. The, INTRODUCTION: TRACING CHANGES, THROUGH A THOUSAND YEARS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that map 1 was made in 1154 CE by the Arab geographer Al-Idrisi.

The text explains that the section reproduced here is a detail of the Indian subcontinent from his larger map of the world.

The text explains that map 2 was made in the 1720s by a French cartographer.

The text explains that the two maps are quite different even though they are of the same area.

The text explains that in al-Idrisi’s map, south India is where we island at the top.

The text explains that place-names are marked in Arabic, Cartographer A person who makes maps.

The text explains that iNTRODUCTION: TRACING CHANGES THROUGH A THOUSAND YEARS Map 1 A section of the world map drawn by the geographer al-Idrisi in the twelfth century showing the Indian subcontinent from land to sea.

The text explains that 2 and there are some well-known names like Kanauj in Uttar Pradesh (spelt in the map as Qanauj).

The text explains that map 2 was made nearly 600 years after Map 1, during this period, information about the subcontinent had changed considerably.

The text explains that this map seems more familiar to us and the coastal areas in particular are surprisingly detailed.

The text explains that this map was used by European sailors and merchants on their voyages.

The text explains that look at the areas in the interior of the subcontinent on Map 2.

The text explains that follow the course of the River Ganga and see how it is shown.

The text explains that why do you think there is a difference in the level of detail and accuracy between the coastal and inland areas in this map?

The text explains that map 2 The subcontinent, from the early-eighteenth- century Atlas Nouveau of Guillaume de l’Isle. ?

The text explains that equally important is the fact that the science of cartography differed in the two periods.

The text explains that as we will see later in the chapter, this was somewhat similar to the way the fourteenth-century poet Amir Khusrau used the word “Hind”.

The text explains that 4 In the medieval period, a “foreigner” was any stranger who appeared say in a given village, someone who was not a part of that society or culture.

The text explains that historians and their Sources Historians use different types of sources to learn about the past depending upon the period of their study example, you read about rulers of the Gupta dynasty and Harshavardhana.

The text explains that you will notice some continuity in the sources used by historians for the study of this period.

The text explains that today all national and state governments have archives where they keep all their and transactions.

The text explains that 2 Different kinds of handwriting could make the reading of Persian and Arabic nastaliq style (on the left) is cursive and easy to read, the shikaste (on the right) is denser

The text explains that you will learn about some of these changes in Chapter 5.

The text explains that as society became more differentiated, people were grouped into or sub-castes and ranked on the basis of their backgrounds and their occupations.

The text explains that region and Empire (see Chapter 2 for an example of a ) praising explained that he was the ruler of a vast empire that stretched from Bengal (Gauda) in the east to Ghazni (Gajjana) in Afghanistan in the west and included all of south India (Dravida).

The text explains that people of different regions – Gauda, Andhra, Kerala, Karnataka, Maharashtra and Map 3 Provinces of the Delhi Sultanate during Muhammad Tughluq’s reign according to the Egyptian source Masalik Mamalik al-Amsar of Shihabuddin Umari.

The text explains that amir Khusrau went on to explain that in contrast to these languages, there was Sanskrit which did not belong to any region.

The text explains that you will learn more about these in Chapter 7.

The text explains that this was apparent in the emergence of many distinct and shared traditions: in the realms of governance, the management of the economy, elite cultures and language.

The text explains that old and New Religions The thousand years of history that we are exploring witnessed major developments in religious traditions.

The text explains that these included the worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society.

The text explains that you will be learning about this, and other traditions, in Chapter 6.

The text explains that in the middle of the nineteenth century, British historians divided the history of India into three periods: “Hindu”, “Muslim” and “British”.

The text explains that these thousand years of Indian history witnessed considerable change.

The text explains that as you read this book, look out for signs of change and the historical processes at work.

The text explains that choose one of the themes mentioned in this chapter, such as economic, social or political history, and discuss why you think history of that theme.

The text explains that (b) The Marathas asserted their political importance during this period.

The text explains that (c) Forest-dwellers were sometimes pushed out of their lands with the spread of agricultural settlements.

The text explains that (a) Archives are places where _______________ are kept.

The text explains that (b) _______________ was a fourteenth-century chronicler.

The text explains that (c) _____, _____, _____, _____ and _____ were some of the new crops introduced into the subcontinent during this period. this period.

The text explains that what were some of the major religious developments during this period?

The text explains that in what ways has the meaning of the term “Hindustan” changed over the centuries?

The text explains that how do historians divide the past into periods?

The text explains that compare either Map 1 or Map 2 with the present-day map of the subcontinent, listing as many similarities 12.

The text explains that find out where records are kept in your village or city.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years, which statement is supported by the source text about 3 INTRODUCTION: TRA...?
options: New and Old Terminologies If the context in which information is produced changes with time, what about language and meanings?
Historical records exist in a variety of languages which have changed considerably over the years.
Medieval Persian, for example, is different from modern Persian.
The difference is not just with regard to grammar and vocabulary; the meanings of words also change over time.
